text: The video shows the polyp located within a diverticulum in the sigmoid colon, visually identified as the 'tick' where the polyp resides.
The surgeon uses cautery to perform a wedge resection of the diverticulum on the anti-mesenteric surface of the colon, entering the bowel lumen.
The surgeon excises the polyp by cutting around the affected area on the anti-mesenteric surface of the colon.
The surgeon holds up a section of the colon with their left hand, indicating the location of the polyp within the tissue.
The surgeon uses cautery to perform a wedge resection of the diverticulum on the anti-mesenteric surface of the colon, ensuring that the bowel beneath the diverticulum is adequately excised.
The surgeon visually identifies the polyp located beneath the left-hand instrument, holding it up for clear observation.
The surgeon observes the bowel wall at the anti-mesenteric surface, noting that it is involved but does not impact the excision process.
The surgeon completes the separation of the specimen from the surrounding tissue, indicating the end of the excision process.
The surgeon utilizes three 5mm laparoscopic ports to perform the procedure, providing access for instruments and visualization within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon prepares for the gastroenterologist to remove the specimen through the colon using a natural orifice approach.
The surgeon examines the defect in the colon, assessing the area where the specimen was previously separated.
The surgeon has come across a distal diverticulum halfway during the procedure, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon stabilizes the bowel with a 3-0 silk suture on the far lateral aspect and divides a bridge to open it to the lumen of the colon, revealing a small defect.
The surgeon creates an opening on the left anterior aspect of the sigmoid colon, revealing the interior lumen.
The surgeon selects a V-lock suture to close the defect in the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon demonstrates the preservation of the sigmoid colon during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon closes the defect on the bowel wall transversely, ensuring the lumen is not narrowed.
The surgeon inserts a needle through a five millimeter trocar, causing a slight bend at the needle tip, but continues suturing the bowel wall without any impact on the procedure.
The surgeon imbricates the bowel wall using an over-and-over suture technique, similar to the closure of a common channel in an intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the closure of the surgical site in two layers, using a silk stitch as a stay suture positioned laterally, and ties the stitch to secure it.
The surgeon inspects the closure site, observing the sutured layers and the silk stay suture tied laterally.
The surgeon performs a scope to inspect the surgical site, checking for any bleeding, ensuring patency, and confirming there is no luminal narrowing.
The endoscope is smoothly inserted through the lumen, indicating a patent passage without obstruction.
